era_context: Identical to 2600 BCE: Early Dynastic Period III in Mesopotamia, Fourth Dynasty Egypt at its height, mature Indus Valley Civilization, and the uncertain early phase of the Xia Dynasty in China. This year falls within a multi-century era of relative stability in Egypt and the Indus Valley, contrasted with increasing militarization in Mesopotamia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (Steppe cultures poorly documented for this period)

events.title: Continuation of Indus Valley Civilization mature phase
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continued its mature phase with no documented major disruptions. Urban centers maintained their standardized planning, trade networks remained active, and the civilization's characteristic material culture persisted. This year is archaeologically indistinguishable from surrounding years in the mature phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE), representing a period of cultural continuity and stability.

sources.name: Archaeological stratigraphy and radiocarbon dating from Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The mature phase of the Indus Valley Civilization is archaeologically confirmed, but individual years within this phase cannot be distinguished without high-precision dating methods.

events.title: Fourth Dynasty Egypt under Khafre or Menkaure
events.description: The Fourth Dynasty continued its reign, with either Khafre (r. c. 2558–2532 BCE) or Menkaure (r. c. 2532–2503 BCE) likely on the throne around 2601 BCE, depending on the chronological system used. The period was characterized by continued monumental construction, including the Pyramid of Khafre and the Great Sphinx at Giza. The Egyptian state maintained its centralized authority and agricultural surplus-based economy.
events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology remains uncertain by ±50–100 years; the specific pharaoh reigning in 2601 BCE cannot be determined with precision.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for 2601 BCE in any tradition. The year is archaeologically indistinguishable from surrounding years. Egyptian chronology remains debated, and no contemporary sources provide year-specific information.
historiographic_note: 2601 BCE is not a year for which any specific historical events are documented. The historical record for this year is entirely dependent on archaeological evidence showing continuity with surrounding years. This reflects the general limitation of ancient chronology: most years in the pre-classical world are archaeologically invisible as discrete units.
